coredata.dc:title: A flow injection fluorescence “turn-on” sensor for the determination of metformin hydrochloride based on the inner filter effect of nitrogen-doped carbon dots/gold nanoparticles double-probe
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Guo-Qi
Zhang, Xu-Yan
Luo, Ya-Xiong
Li, Yong-Sheng
Zhao, Yang
Gao, Xiu-Feng
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, an ultrasensitive and rapid “turn-on” fluorescence sensor, integrating flow-injection (FI) with nitrogen-doped carbon dots/gold nanoparticles (N-CDs/AuNPs) double-probe is established for the determination of metformin hydrochloride (MET) in biological fluids. The sensing strategy involves the weak inner filter effect between AuNPs and N-CDs due to aggregation products of MET with AuNPs. Unfortunately, the degree of AuNPs aggregation is difficult to control through manual assays, resulting in intolerable measurement error that limits further applications. However, the proposed method overcomes the above problem, and significantly lowers the consumption of expensive reagents (AuNPs: about 60 μL per test). Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity at 400 nm excitation and 505 nm emission wavelengths display a linear correlation with MET concentration (5–100 μg L−1) and the limit of detection is 2.32 μg L−1 (3.3 S/k). The advantages of the presented method include high sensitivity, rapid speed (60 sample h−1), good accuracy and precision (RSD ≤ 2.1%, n = 11) and low cost. Since MET is the first-line hypoglycemic agent in patients with type II diabetes, this method can preliminarily determine MET content in urine samples, giving satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Gold nanoparticles
Fluorescence
Metformin
Flow injection

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Behavior of AuNPs on fluorescence decrease of N-CDs
The IFE of AuNPs and N-CDs
Effect of merging modes between N-CDs and AuNPs/MET
Effect of AuNPs and N-CDs concentrations
Determination of MET in urine samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the double-probe mechanism for assaying MET.

The TEM images of N-CDs/AuNPs (A) and N-CDs/AuNPs/MET (B). N-CDs/AuNPs/MET. The experimental conditions as follow: the AuNPs concentration is 8 nM; the N-CDs concentration is 0.7 mg·L−1; the MET concentration is 100 μg·L−1.

FI-FP-MET manifold for the determination of MET content in urine. C1: ultrapure water carrier 1; C2: ultrapure water carrier 2; RC1: reaction coil 1; RC2: reaction coil 2; P1: peristaltic pump 1; P2: peristaltic pump 2; R: reagent loop; F-D: flow-through fluorescence detector; S: sample loop; W: waste.

Characterization of N-CDs. (A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs obtained at different excitation wavelengths in 380–450 nm range; (B) Excitation and emission spectra, and UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs; inset shows pictures of N-CDs under visible light (left) and UV light (right); (C) TEM image of N-CDs; inset shows size distribution histogram of N-CDs. (D) FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs. In the above figure, the concentration of N-CDs was 0.7 mg L−1.

(A) Fluorescent spectra of N-CDs containing different concentrations AuNPs (0–9 nM). (B) Fluorescent kinetics of N-CDs/AuNPs+ and N-CDs/AuNPs−; (C) Fluorescent emission spectra of N-CDs, N-CDs/AuNPs and N-CDs/AuNPs/MET; MET concentration was 20 μg L−1 (curve 4) and 100 μg L−1 (curve 5), respectively; (D) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs and absorption spectra of AuNPs in aggregated (100 μg L−1 MET) and dispersive states. In the above figure, the concentration of N-CDs was 0.7 mg L−1 and concentration of AuNPs was 8 nM.

Evaluation of FP-FI-MET system performance. Experimental conditions are as follows: the excitation and emission wavelengths are 400 and 505 nm, respectively; RC1 and RC2 are 80 and 120 cm, respectively; AuNPs concentration is 8 nM; N-CDs concentration is 0.7 mg L−1; the volumes of R1, R2, and S are 60, 120, and 120 μL, respectively.
Determination of MET in human urine samples.
Comparison of analytical methods for the determination of MET.

head.ce:title: A flow injection fluorescence “turn-on” sensor for the determination of metformin hydrochloride based on the inner filter effect of nitrogen-doped carbon dots/gold nanoparticles double-probe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: West China School of Basic Medical Sciences & Forensic Medicine, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610041, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: West China School of Basic Medical Sciences & Forensic Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: West China School of Basic Medical Sciences & Forensic Medicine, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610041, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, an ultrasensitive and rapid “turn-on” fluorescence sensor, integrating flow-injection (FI) with nitrogen-doped carbon dots/gold nanoparticles (N-CDs/AuNPs) double-probe is established for the determination of metformin hydrochloride (MET) in biological fluids. The sensing strategy involves the weak inner filter effect between AuNPs and N-CDs due to aggregation products of MET with AuNPs. Unfortunately, the degree of AuNPs aggregation is difficult to control through manual assays, resulting in intolerable measurement error that limits further applications. However, the proposed method overcomes the above problem, and significantly lowers the consumption of expensive reagents (AuNPs: about 60 μL per test). Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity at 400 nm excitation and 505 nm emission wavelengths display a linear correlation with MET concentration (5–100 μg L−1) and the limit of detection is 2.32 μg L−1 (3.3 S/k). The advantages of the presented method include high sensitivity, rapid speed (60 sample h−1), good accuracy and precision (RSD ≤ 2.1%, n = 11) and low cost. Since MET is the first-line hypoglycemic agent in patients with type II diabetes, this method can preliminarily determine MET content in urine samples, giving satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes is a global disease that seriously threatens the health of patients due to hyperglycemia. The latest report released by International Diabetes Federation shows that people suffering from diabetes is increasing yearly, this foreshadows that the number of diabetics will reach 700 million by 2045, which is about 1/13 adults. Metformin hydrochloride (MET) can decrease blood glucose concentration via the inhibition of liver uptake of lactic acid and enhancement of insulin performance. Therefore, MET is the first choice for diabetic patients owing to its safety and high efficiency [1,2]. In recent years, MET novel effects toward anti-cancer and anti-aging have attracted increasing attention [3,4]. Clinical studies have shown that 70% of MET in medicines are excreted through urine [5]. Due to MET inability to bind with plasma proteins, accumulation of MET in the body can initiate side effects such as lactic acidosis [6]. Therefore, it is very important for sensitive monitoring of MET exclusion in order to benefit diabetic patients, in which sampling of urine is convenient and safe approach.

Scientists have reported several methods for the determination of MET, in which high-performance liquid chromatography-ultraviolet (HPLC-UV) [7–9] is the most commonly examined; however, it is limited by a complex and time-consuming system. Although the rapid-speed associated with the ultra-HPLC-UV [10] method is a marked improvement, the necessary devices and exclusive accessories are expensive. LC-mass spectrometric method [11] is sensitive and accurate, but its apparatus is unsuitable for popularization and application. Other approaches to sensitive and simple determination of MET include capillary electrophoresis [12], electrochemical [13], colorimetric [14,15] and near infrared spectroscopy [16]. However, the fluorescent method is considered more compactable for detecting MET, due to advantages such as simplicity, good selectivity and fast response.

Nanotechnology in analytical chemistry is under rapid development, with carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) receiving considerable attention in various fields, such as ions [17,18], catalysis [19], cell imaging [20], proteins [21], dopamine [22], DNA [23], etc. Compared with organic fluorescent dyes and metal quantum dots, CDs possess photo-stability, water solubility, functionality and low toxicity [24]. It is worth noting that AuNPs have the ability to recognize target molecules, where their state changes from dispersion to aggregation along with the color [25]. On the other hand, AuNPs are also ideal fluorescent absorber due to the high molar extinction coefficient [26]. A large overlap between emission/absorption spectra of the donor/acceptor will produce an inner filter effect (IFE) [27]. When AuNPs are induced to aggregate and change color by target molecules, this will weaken the efficiency of IFE between AuNPs and CDs. The fluorescence method developed based on IFE has the advantage of high sensitivity and lower detection limit. Meanwhile, the IFE also has the advantages of simple operation and fast response [28]. Therefore, using the above principle, various “turn-on” fluorescent sensors for assaying various targets have been proposed, such as dopamine [29], acetamiprid [30], bisphenol A [31] and glutathione [32]. However, few reports have described AuNPs only as a quencher, and the effects of AuNPs aggregation on fluorescence intensity have yet to be examined. The lack to research in this area arises from difficulty in manually controlling AuNPs dynamic aggregation [33]. Owing to the sensitivity of fluorescence, a multitude of errors can occur, which limits future applications and promotion.

Flow-injection analysis (FI) [34,35] can be connected with various detectors and can complement many fields, such as industrial monitoring [36], food safety [37], bioassay [38,39], and other fields [40,41]. FI can superiorly control the chemical reaction to obtain reliable results under non-equilibrium flowing state, which can resolve the difficulty of controlling AuNPs aggregation.

Herein, an ultrasensitive, rapid and accurate CDs-AuNPs-FI fluorometry for the determination of MET was developed by introducing CDs and AuNPs to FI. The proposed method accurately controls the agglomeration degree of AuNPs and improves analysis accuracy. Furthermore, the closed FI system can avoid the fluorescence quenching caused by oxygen in air. Currently, few reports have explored the use of fluorescence methodology for MET detection. Therefore, we studied AuNPs-CDs-FI fluorometry and its application for the determination of MET content in human urine.

Pure MET was acquired from Bangtai chemistry Co., Ltd. (Jiangsu, China); Au(III) chloride trihydrate was purchased from Maisike Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Tianjing, China); Melamine was purchased from Anpu Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Tianjing, China); Polyethyleneimine (PEI) was purchased by Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (ShangHai, China); Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was purchased by Sanland Chemical Co., Ltd. (Los Angeles, USA); NaCO3, NaHCO3, HCl, HNO3, citric acid and sodium citrate were purchased from Kelong chemistry Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Uric acid and creatinine purchased from BoMei Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (HeFei, China); All chemicals used in the study were of analytical reagent grade.

The fluorescence spectra were obtained using RF-5301PC-type fluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), and F96s-type fluorophotometer (Lengguang, China) was used for continuously detection. Absorption spectra and absorbance were obtained using UV-1800-PC-type spectrophotometer (MeiPuDa, China). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were conducted using Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA), which was operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. K-Alpha+ (Thermo Scientific, USA) were utilized for X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR) spectroscopic data were generated using a Spectrum-two-Li10014-type FTIR (Perkin Elmer, USA). Zeta potential was analyzed with a Zetasizer Nano Zs 90 (Malvern, UK). The FI system consisted of FIA-3100 type flow-injection processor (Jitian, China) and spectrophotometer. Pretreatment of the uric samples was achieved using TGW16-type high-speed centrifuge (Yingtai, China). Purified water used in all experiments was produced using an AKL-UP-IV-10-type water purification system (Kangning, China).

Doped-nitrogen atoms not only increase the quantum yield of pure CDs, but also provide additional binding sites of the related reactions. Therefore, nitrogen-rich melamine was selected as the nitrogen source in this study. First, citric acid (5.0 g) was dissolved in pure water (2 mL) with stirring and heating (120 °C). Then melamine solution (5 mL, 1.4 g L−1) was added drop-wise over a 2 min period, and stirred under heating for 35 min. When the mixture changed from colorless to yellow colloid, it was cooled to room temperature, followed by the addition of pure water (50 mL). Finally, dialysis (Molecular-weight cut-off: 100–500 Dalton) was carried out for 24 h to remove residue species. The solution was centrifuged at 4500 rpm for 10 min and the supernatant was gathered. Ultimately, the concentration of the nitrogen-doped carbon dot (N-CDs) was defined as 0.1 g L−1. For comparison, pure CDs without doped-nitrogen were prepared under the same conditions.

Preparation of 50 μg·L−1 MET working: pure MET (10 mg) was dissolved in HCl (100 mL, 1 mM), which was used as the stock solution, from which 50 μL of the stock solution was transferred to a 100 mL volumetric flask and diluted to the mark with HCl (1 mM) solution.

Preparation of 0.7 mg·L−1N-CDs: N-CDs solution (5 mL, 0.1 g·L−1) was mixed with pure water (15 mL) and NaOH (15 mL, 20 g·L−1) to adjust the pH to neutral. Then, 3.75 mL of neutral N-CDs was transferred to a 100 mL volumetric flask and diluted to the mark with Na2CO3-NaHCO3 buffer (0.1 mM, pH 9).

Negatively charged AuNPs− were prepared using the classical Frens method [42]. Briefly, HAuCl4 solution (100 mL, 1 mM) was heated to boiling under vigorous stirring under reflux, and trisodium citrate solution (10 mL, 38.8 mM) was quickly added changing the color from light yellow to red. The mixture was continuously boiled for 15 min. Finally, AuNPs solution was filtered with 0.45 μm filter paper and the filtrate was stored at 4 °C. The particle size and concentration of synthesized AuNPs were determined as 10 nM [43] (Fig. S1A) and 13 nm (Fig. S1B), respectively. The prepared AuNPs were stable under acidic conditions with pH range of 2~6 (Fig. S1C). Furthermore, the surface potential of the AuNPs was measured and the zeta potential value was −32.9 mV (Fig. S1D).

The principle of the ultrasensitive N-CDs/AuNPs dual-probe method for MET is shown in Fig. 1 . In alkaline condition, N-CDs synthesized by citric acid and melamine contain amino groups on its surface and are negatively charged. When AuNPs− was added to N-CD solution, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs showed a significant decrease, due to IFE arising from the dispersed AuNPs and N-CDs. Then, the addition of positively charged acidic MET (protonation) solution into the N-CDs/AuNPs solution, MET+ was adsorbed on the surface of AuNPs− causing AuNPs to aggregate. This changes the color of N-CDs/AuNPs solution. Thus, IFE weakens and the fluorescence signal was stronger. Hence, the recovery degree of the fluorescence was closely related to the aggregation degree of AuNPs. The concentration of MET+ related to the degree of AuNPs aggregation (Fig. S2), and corresponded to the N-CDs fluorescence emission via the IFE between N-CDs and AuNPs.

In addition, TEM images of N-CDs/AuNPs and N-CDs/AuNPs/MET are shown in Fig. 2 . The results show that N-CDs and AuNPs were dispersed in solution (Fig. 2A). Then, the target MET was adsorbed on the surface of AuNPs rather than N-CDs (Fig. 2B).

A schematic diagram of the proposed flow-injection fluorospectrophotometry system with N-CDs/AuNPs dual-probe for the determination of MET (FI-FP-MET) is shown in Fig. 3 . The system includes two peristaltic pumps (P), a multifunction injection valve, two reaction coils (RC), a flow-through fluorescence detector (F-D) and a computer which recorded and processed data. The inner diameter of the PTFE connecting pipe is 5 mm. The procedures for determining MET are as follows:
The zone of AuNPs and MET are first injected into carrier 1 to form the MET/AuNPs reaction products in RC1. Simultaneously, the N-CDs reagent probe is filled into the FI-FP-MET system.
By automatically switching the valve, the MET/AuNPs product and N-CDs are mixed at the Tee, and then the fluorescence of N-CDs is quenched in RC2. The fluorescence signal is recorded using F-D.
Moreover, the schematic diagram in inset Fig. 3 shows the procedure for determining MET in the FI-FP-MET system.

The fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs were used to study N-CDs probe optical performance. The fluorescence emission peaks of N-CDs did not shift under different excitation wavelengths between 380 and 450 nm (Fig. 4 A). Hence, N-CDs display excitation-independent fluorescence behavior. Fig. 4B shows that N-CDs has a UV–vis characteristic absorption peak at approx. 350 nm, corresponding to the transition of n-π* (CO) [44]. As shown in Fig. 4B insert), the N-CDs solution displays a transparent faint yellow color under sunlight, and blue-green color under UV light at 365 nm.

The TEM images of N-CDs solution reveal the morphology and particle size distribution (Fig. 4C). N-CDs are spherical in shape, mono-dispersive with a dispersive range of 3.6–14.4 nm, and good uniformity of size, with a mean size of about 7 nm (Fig. 4C insert). After freeze-drying, the surface functional groups of N-CDs were characterized by FT-IR analysis. As shown in Fig. 4D, the absorption peak of N-CDs at 1384 nm−1 relates to the bending stretching vibration of OH, and the peak at 1703 nm−1 relates to the stretching vibration of CO. Hence, N-CDs consist of citric acid, which has abundant hydroxyl and carbonyl groups. In addition, the FT-IR spectra also show a strong absorption peak at 3405 and 1622 nm−1, corresponding to the stretching and bending stretching vibrations of NH, respectively. As shown in Fig. S3A, XPS survey spectrum exhibits three typical peaks attributed to C 1 s (At% 20.00%), N 1s (At% 33.33%) and O 1s (At% 46.67%), respectively. Fig. S3B shows the high-resolution C 1s spectrum, where three peaks at 284.7, 285.3 and 286.5 eV are observed, corresponding to CC/CC, CN and CO, respectively. The above results show that the N atom has been doped into CDs producing N-CDs. Finally, the calculated results of the fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of N-CDs and pure CDs (Table S1) show that doping nitrogen can improve QY of N-CDs [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Behavior of AuNPs on fluorescence decrease of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The evaluation of AuNPs− influence on reducing the fluorescent intensity of N-CDs was examined to elucidate the assay principle of MET. Therefore, the effects of AuNPs− concentration on N-CDs fluorescence was studied (Fig. 5 A). When the concentration of AuNPs− increases in N-CDs/AuNPs− mixture solution, the fluorescence gradually decreases, which indicates that AuNPs− weakens N-CDs fluorescence.

Furthermore, the effect of surface charge on fluorescence weakening was investigated. To determine the surface charge, N-CDs solution was allowed to pass through a cationic exchange resin column, and the fluorescent value of the effluent was monitored at 400 nm excitation and 505 nm emission wavelengths (Fig. S4). There was no interaction with the cation-exchange resins on the ion-exchange column, proving that N-CDs− is negatively charged. Furthermore, the above experimental results are confirmed by the zeta potential value (−8.9 mV) of N-CDs (Fig. S5).

The effect of the prepared AuNPs+ surface charge was further investigated using the PEI reduction method described in ref [46]. The fluorescent kinetics curves of N-CDs−/AuNPs+ and N-CDs−/AuNPs+ mixture solutions are shown in Fig. 5B. The results suggest that when N-CDs− is mixed with AuNPs− in equal volumes, its fluorescence intensity rapidly decreases (I segment), and then remains at a constant over time. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs− mixed with AuNPs+ displays a rapid decrease (II segment), and continuously decreases over time.

This phenomenon was further elaborated by comparing the absorption spectra of AuNPs+ and AuNPs− with the fluorescence spectrum of N-CDs− (Fig. S6A). The strong absorption band at 400–550 nm of AuNPs+ and AuNPs− are similar, but the absorption band partially overlaps with the fluorescent peak of N-CDs− at 505 nm. Hence, the reason for the fluorescent decrease of “I segment“ in Fig. 5B is attributed to IFE. The comparison of the absorption spectra of N-CDs−/AuNPs+ and N-CDs−/AuNPs− mixtures, reveal that N-CDs−/AuNPs+ possesses an additional absorption peak at 600–700 nm, which indicates that N-CDs− with AuNPs+ forms chromogenic agglomeration (600–700 nm) due to electrostatic interaction (Fig. S6B). Therefore, the further fluorescent decrease in ”II segment“ is a result of AuNPs+ agglomerating with N-CDs−.

Due to the slow agglomeration process of N-CDs−/AuNPs+, the fluorescence affected gradually decreases. Protonated MET+ is positively charged under acidic conditions; hence, it can combine with AuNPs− via electrostatic interaction to release fluorescent N-CDs− from the N-CDs−/AuNPs+ agglomerate, resulting in a distinctive intense fluorescence of MET+/AuNPs− and N-CDs− mixture. Therefore, N-CDs− and AuNPs− probes were used for subsequent studies in the analytical system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The IFE of AuNPs and N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of MET on the IFE of N-CDs/AuNPs was investigated under the same conditions (Fig. 5C). N-CDs solution displays a distinct fluorescence peak at 505 nm (curve 1). Upon mixing MET with N-CDs, this fluorescence peak remains unaffected (curve 2). However, with the addition of a certain amount of AuNPs to N-CDs, the fluorescence peak shows a visible decrease (curve 3), which is attributed to AuNPs absorbing the fluorescence of N-CDs, due to the IFE. When N-CDs is firstly added to AuNPs, and mixed with different concentrations of MET, a part of the fluorescence is shielded and that absorbed by AuNPs particles can be released (curve 4 and 5).

Furthermore, to examine the mechanism in which AuNPs affects the fluorescence of N-CDs, the dispersed and aggregated states of AuNPs absorption spectra were scanned, and then integrated with the fluorescence spectrum of the N-CDs (Fig. 5D). In contrast to the dispersion of AuNPs (curve 3), the absorption curve of aggregated AuNPs is at approx. 500–600 nm (curve 2), where the IFE is reduced. Therefore, aggregated AuNPs weakens its capacity to absorb the fluorescence of N-CDs, and the relationship between the amount of aggregated AuNPs− induced by MET and fluorescence recovery of N-CDs can be used to quantify MET.

A flow-injection fluorescence system for the determination of MET (FI-FP-MET) in biological fluids was designed (Fig. 3 inset). The MET sample and AuNPs reagent through a side by side permeating form were injected into the carrier generating a permeating zone of MET and AuNPs, where part of the dispersed AuNPs was agglomerated. Then, the injected N-CDs zone, originating from another thin pipeline, merged with the MET/AuNPs zone producing the N-CDs/MET/AuNPs product zone.

The initial system conditions were as follows: Purified water was used as the carriers (C1, C2); the reaction coils (RC1, RC2) were 100 cm; the loop volumes (R1, S, R2) were 40, 80 and 100 μL, respectively; AuNPs concentration was 6 nM; N-CDs concentration was 0.1 mg L−1; the excitation and emission wavelengths were 400 and 505 nm, respectively; the fluorescent pass-band was 10 × 15 nm, the total flow rate was 1.5 mL min−1; and experiments were carried out at room temperature. The experiments shown in Fig. 5C were repeated twice to verify the applicability of the system. The obtained results showed that the phenomenon of “turn-on” fluorescence could be realized in the FI-FP-MET system (Fig. S7).

When purified water, and AuNPs and N-CDs reagents were injected into the FI-FP-MET system and the obtained fluorescent intensity (F0) was used as the blank. Upon injecting the MET solution as the sample into the system, fluorescent intensity (F) was obtained. Finally, ΔF (F - F0) was used to quantify the MET concentration and optimize parameters of the FI-FP-MET system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of merging modes between N-CDs and AuNPs/MET
ce:section.ce:para: In the system, there are three merging zone modes between MET/AuNPs and N-CDs through adjusting the time of the second step in “FI-FP-MET system and determination procedure”, which is the loop-loading time of N-CDs (R2). Clearly, different merging modes influence the assay sensitivity of MET by changing the time of N-CDs (Fig. S8). When the loop-loading time of N-CDs is changed from 7 s to 9 s, the ΔF increases, but decreases after 9 s. Hence, the merging mode changes from I and II to III (Fig. S8 insert). At 9 s, the N-CDs zone totally enwraps the MET/AuNPs zone, which is mode II (Fig. S8 insert). Consequently, the optimal time of N-CDs was 9 s.

The influence of injection volume of MET and AuNPs on ΔF for quantifying MET. Upon increasing the injection volume of MET, ΔF shows an increasing trend and then plateaus after 120 μL (Fig. S9A). Furthermore, when the injection volume of AuNPs is 60 μL, a consistent effect is observed for MET determination (Fig. S9B). Therefore, 120 μL and 60 μL were used as the injection volumes of MET and AuNPs, respectively. Additionally, if the injection volume of N-CDs is insufficient, the MET/AuNPs zone cannot be fully wrapped, hence, the injection volume of N-CDs was also 120 μL.

The FI system’s reactor is reaction coils (RC1, RC2), and its length not only controls the reaction process but also affects sample throughput. In the system, the agglomeration of MET-induced AuNPs occurs in RC1 and the reaction at the MET/AuNPs zone diminishes the fluorescence of N-CDs in RC2. Therefore, the influence of RC1 or RC2 on MET determination was investigated in the range of 30–120 cm, respectively.

When RC1 and RC2 length is increased, the ΔF of reaction product increases and then decreases (Fig. S9C and S9D). Within the reaction coils, the reaction product of the N-CDs/MET/AuNPs zone is both generated and diluted. If the reaction coil length is too short and the rate of product formation is greater than that of dilution, then ΔF increases with increasing coil length. When the length exceeds the optimal value, the product’s fluorescence displays a downward trend. Therefore, 80 and 120 cm were optimal lengths for RC1 and RC2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of AuNPs and N-CDs concentrations
ce:section.ce:para: Optimization of the concentration of N-CDs and AuNPs for the determination of MET shows that N-CDs concentration in the system greatly affects ΔF of the N-CDs/MET/AuNPs product (Fig. S9E). When the concentration increases to 0.7 mg L−1, ΔF reaches its maximum, and then gradually decreases. Therefore, optimal N-CDs concentration was determined as 0.7 mg L−1.

Furthermore, when AuNPs concentration increases to 8 nM, ΔF of the product gradually increases (Fig. S9F), therefore, excessive dispersion of AuNPs in the system can further weaken the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs due to the IFE of AuNPs particles. Thus, 8 nM AuNPs concentration was optimal.

According to the above results, the optimized FP-FI-MET system conditions are as follows: the excitation and emission wavelengths are 400 and 505 nm, respectively; RC1 and RC2 are 80 and 120 cm, respectively; AuNPs concentration is 8 nM; N-CDs concentration is 0.7 mg L−1; the volumes of R1, R2, and S are 60, 120, and 120 μL, respectively.

Under optimized conditions, the MET standards of 10 and 60 mg L−1 were used to examine the precision of the FP-FI-MET system. The MET standards were repeatedly injected into the FP-FI-MET system (n = 11) to detect the fluorescence intensity. The obtained curves are shown in Fig. 6 (left). The calculated relative standard deviation (RSD) of the system is below 2.06%, which proves the robustness of the designed system.

To evaluate the linearity of the system, the MET standards of varied concentrations were used as testing samples (Fig. 6 (right)). When the concentrations of MET are between 5 and 100 μg L−1, the linear regression equation is as follows: ΔF = 2.53 C MET − 6.32; then, based on the 3.3 S/k rule (S = 1.78 F, k = 2.53 F L μg−1) [47], the detection limit of the system is 2.32 μg L−1.

Finally, to verify the anti-interference ability of this system and method, potential interferences of other molecules (glucose, urea, uric acid, creatinine, cysteine, sodium ascorbate and sodium ascorbate), and ions (K+, Na+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Cl−, NO3 −, CH3COO− and SO4 2−) were injected into the system to evaluate their effects. The results show that no interfere is observed when assaying MET (Fig. S10),

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of MET in urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: Since the collected urine samples did not contain MET, they were taken as the sample matrix, for the recovery experiment of the standard addition to demonstrate the applicability of the system and method in practical analysis. The spiked samples were prepared by adding 200 μL of precipitation solution (acetonitrile) to 50 μL of urine sample and then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min to remove impurities. The supernatant was directly injected into the FI-FP-MET system.

Table 1 shows that the obtained recoveries are between 90.8 and 107.8% with RSD < 0.67%. Hence, the designed system possesses excellent accuracy and reproducibility, and can be used for rapid detection of MET in actual samples.

Table 2 compares the designed assay for MET detection with published ones. Our system displays higher sensitivity, faster analysis, greener and less cost, and more suitable for MET quantification in biological fluid and practical promotion applications.

A flow injection “turn-on” fluorescence sensor for the MET determination based on the N-CDs/AuNPs double-probe was established. AuNPs at different aggregate states were used for the development of a rapid and accurate flow-injection fluorescence system. The measurement errors in the AuNPs/CDs assay were greatly improved. Additionally, a simple synthetic method for N-CDs preparation with high fluorescence quantum yield was established. Finally, the method was successfully employed to MET determination in urine samples, and shows potential applications in the health sector.

Guo-Qi Zhang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Xu-Yan Zhang: Methodology, Investigation. Ya-Xiong Luo: Software, Visualization. Yong-Sheng Li: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Yang Zhao: Software. Xiu-Feng Gao: Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.119384.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metformin-Mechanisms of action and use for the treatment of type 2 diabetes mellitus

D. Kirpichnikov, S.I. Mcfarlane, J.R. Sowers, Metformin: An Update, Ann. Intern. Med. 137 (2002) 25-33. https://doi.org/10.7326/0003-4819-137-1-200207020-00009.
